data.title: Assassin's Creed Maker Ubisoft Weighs Around 200 Job Cuts in France
data.description:
By Mauro Orru

Ubisoft Entertainment is considering eliminating about 200 jobs at its headquarters in France through voluntary termination agreements as the group seeks to cut costs.

The French videogame group said it had entered into discussions with trade unions over the proposal, which, if approved, would only affect Ubisoft International employees with French contracts. Ubisoft International counts some 1,100 workers.

The company said roles would be eliminated based on a collective, voluntary mutual termination agreement, meaning that no employees would be forced out. The proposal will require a deal with trade unions and validation by French authorities.

Last week, Ubisoft said it was embarking on a major structural overhaul, discontinuing and postponing several games, closing some studios and cutting financial targets as it seeks to revive its fortunes in an increasingly competitive market.

Write to Mauro Orru at mauro.orru@wsj.com

data.title: Triller Group Inc. SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
Triller Group Inc., a diversified technology platform company, has released its annual Form 10-K report, detailing its financial performance, business operations, strategic initiatives, and the challenges it faces. The report provides a comprehensive overview of the company's activities over the past year, highlighting significant developments and future outlooks in the digital content and financial services sectors.

Financial Highlights

  • Total Revenue: $27.5 million, a decrease of 49.30% from the previous year, attributed to economic recession and outward migration in Hong Kong.
  • Net Loss: $1,138.0 million, an increase of 2,212.80% compared to the previous year, primarily due to increased operating expenses and other expenses.
  • Operating Expenses: $140.7 million, a significant increase driven by higher personnel and benefit expenses, legal and professional fees, and impairment charges.
  • Personnel and Benefit Expenses: $84.9 million, increased due to stock-based compensation related to the settlement of accrued salaries and amortization of restricted share units.
  • Legal and Professional Fees: $22.4 million, increased due to higher US legal counsel fees and consulting fees.
  • Other Expense, Net: $1,024.9 million, primarily due to impairment on goodwill and intangible assets.
  • Cash and Cash Equivalents: $3.1 million, indicating limited liquidity to meet planned obligations for the next 12 months.

Business Highlights

  • Technology Platform Expansion: The company has significantly expanded its Technology Platform through organic growth and strategic acquisitions, becoming a diversified platform for the creation, distribution, measurement, and monetization of digital, live, and virtual content.
  • Triller App: The Triller app, a short-form video app similar to TikTok, has established over 327 million Consumer Accounts, with a total of 436 million Consumer Accounts on the Technology Platform. The app leverages AI and ML technologies to enable users to create professional-looking videos.
  • Geographical Performance: The company operates within the global digital content marketplace, focusing on the $250 billion creator economy. It also has a strong presence in the Greater Bay Area, a major financial services market, and is expanding its footprint in Mainland China.
  • Healthcare and Fintech Businesses: The company operates a leading wealth management and healthcare institution in Hong Kong, servicing over 400,000 individual and corporate customers. It also has a market leadership position in the healthcare business through a 4% stake in HCMPS.
  • Platform and Distribution Businesses: The company has implemented a strategy to expand its broker-dealer business into a platform and distribution business, offering a tech-enabled broker management platform for advisors and a portfolio of wealth and health products.
  • Strategic Partnerships: The company is in active discussions to establish a strategic partnership with a top asset manager in China to provide offshore insurance solutions, which could significantly increase its assets under management and competitiveness.
  • Future Outlook: The company plans to leverage its integrated global platform to maximize growth potential, continue investing in AI-powered tools, and expand its experiential offerings to create revenue opportunities and build its brand and culture.

Strategic Initiatives

  • Acquisition of Triller Corp.: The company completed the acquisition of Triller Corp., integrating its operations into the Group, which now includes social media and sports streaming segments. This strategic move aims to diversify the company's offerings and expand its presence in the digital content marketplace.
  • Expansion in Financial Services: The company has been expanding its financial services business, particularly in the Greater Bay Area, through strategic partnerships and investments in FinTech, WealthTech, and HealthTech sectors.
  • Capital Management: The company raised $420 million in capital since inception and has been actively managing its capital structure through various funding alternatives, including external borrowings and public equity. In 2024, the company received $24 million from financing activities, primarily through advances from stockholders and proceeds from convertible debts. The company also engaged in private placements, raising approximately $5.1 million in 2024 and planning a $14 million private placement in 2025. The company has not paid dividends and intends to retain earnings to fund growth and repay indebtedness.
  • Future Outlook: The company anticipates continued operating losses as it invests in business growth. It plans to focus on expanding its distribution footprint and exploring partnerships in Mainland China. The management is committed to improving liquidity and capital resources through revenue growth and expenditure control, while also considering potential fundraising initiatives to support business development and operational expenses. The company aims to leverage its strategic assets to capitalize on emerging opportunities in life insurance, wealth management, and healthcare.

Challenges and Risks

  • Regulatory Risks: The company faces significant regulatory risks due to its operations in Hong Kong and potential expansion into mainland China. The evolving legal landscape in the PRC, including data security, anti-monopoly, and overseas listing regulations, poses uncertainties that could materially impact the company's business operations and financial condition. The PRC government's influence over Hong Kong regulators could indirectly affect the company, especially concerning capital requirements and cybersecurity measures.
  • HFCA Act Compliance: The Holding Foreign Companies Accountable Act (HFCA Act) presents a risk of delisting if the company's auditor is not subject to PCAOB inspections for two consecutive years. Although the company's current auditor is not affected, future regulatory changes could impact compliance and access to U.S. capital markets.
  • Economic and Political Risks: The company is also exposed to economic and political risks associated with emerging markets, particularly China. Adverse changes in economic conditions, government policies, or geopolitical tensions could negatively affect the company's business and prospects.
  • Management's Response: Management acknowledges the challenges posed by the rapidly changing regulatory environment in China and Hong Kong. The company is monitoring developments closely and is prepared to adjust its operations to comply with new regulations. However, the unpredictability of these changes remains a significant concern.
  • Market Risks: The company is exposed to market risks, including fluctuations in the global financial markets and economic conditions. Disruptions in these markets could adversely affect the company's financial condition and its ability to operate effectively. The ongoing geopolitical tensions, such as the conflict between Russia and Ukraine, add to the uncertainty and potential volatility in the markets.

SEC Filing: Triller Group Inc. [ AGBA ] - 10-K - Jan. 26, 2026

data.title: Germany's GEA Group preliminary FY revenue grows 3.7%, hits guidance
data.description:
Overview

  • Germany-based systems supplier's preliminary FY 2025 revenue grew 3.7%, at upper end of guidance

  • Preliminary EBITDA before restructuring expenses rose to EUR 907 mln, EBITDA margin at 16.5%

  • Preliminary order intake for FY 2025 at EUR 5.9 bln, organic growth of 9.1%

Outlook

  • GEA expects organic sales growth of more than 5% for fiscal year 2026

  • Company aims for further margin improvements in 2026

  • GEA CEO says strong order intake supports goal of 5% annual organic sales growth

Result Drivers

  • ORDER INTAKE - Preliminary order intake in Q4 exceeded market expectations, contributing to 9.1% organic growth for FY 2025

  • MARGIN IMPROVEMENT - EBITDA margin before restructuring expenses reached 16.5%, slightly above guidance

  • SALES GROWTH - Full year sales grew 3.7%, at the upper end of guidance

Key Details

Metric

Beat/Miss

Actual

Consensus Estimate

FY Revenue

EUR 5.50 bln

FY Adjusted EBITDA

EUR 907 mln

FY Orders

EUR 5.90 bln

Analyst Coverage

  • The current average analyst rating on the shares is "hold" and the breakdown of recommendations is 8 "strong buy" or "buy", 7 "hold" and 4 "sell" or "strong sell"

  • The average consensus recommendation for the industrial machinery & equipment peer group is "buy."

  • Wall Street's median 12-month price target for GEA Group AG is €64.00, about 5.7% above its January 23 closing price of €60.55

  • The stock recently traded at 19 times the next 12-month earnings vs. a P/E of 20 three months ago

For questions concerning the data in this report, contact Estimates.Support@lseg.com. For any other questions or feedback, contact RefinitivNewsSupport@thomsonreuters.com.

data.title: CVR Energy Falls on Expected 4Q Loss
data.description:
By Elias Schisgall

Shares of CVR Energy fell after the company said it expects to report a loss for the fourth quarter of 2025.

Shares were down 7.4% to $22.73 late Monday morning. The stock has gained 12% in the past 12 months.

The energy holding company said Monday that it expects a loss attributable to stockholders of between $105 million and $120 million in the fourth quarter, compared with a profit of $28 million in the same period a year earlier. Analysts polled by FactSet were expecting a loss of $72 million.

Chief Executive Mark Pytosh attributed the loss in part to accelerated depreciation associated with the reversion of the company's Wynnewood renewable diesel unit back to hydrocarbon processing.

Pytosh said that lower nitrogen fertilizer production and sales due to delays in the turnaround of the Coffeyville fertilizer facility contributed to the loss.

"The reversion of the renewable diesel unit was completed in December, and we are optimistic about the benefits we are already seeing by having the hydrocracker at Wynnewood returned to hydrocarbon processing," Pytosh said. "We also look forward to a year with no planned turnarounds in our Petroleum segment in 2026."

The company said it expects to report earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization for the quarter of $40 million.

For the full year ended Dec. 31, the company said it expects to report a profit of between $17 million and $32 million and ebitda of between $580 million and $600 million.

Write to Elias Schisgall at elias.schisgall@wsj.com

data.title: These Stocks Are Today's Movers: USA Rare Earth, CoreWeave, Micron, Intel, Newmont, IonQ, Sarepta, and More — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Mackenzie Tatananni, George Glover and Joe Woelfel

Stock futures traded mixed Monday after falling earlier. President Donald Trump over the weekend threatened to impose additional tariffs on Canada, fueling fears that the "sell America" trade could gather pace, and the chances for a partial U.S. government shutdown increased.

These stocks were poised to make moves:

USA Rare Earth jumped 20% to $29.85. The Trump administration is planning to inject $1.6 billion into the miner and will receive a 10% stake, the Financial Times reported on Saturday, citing people familiar with the matter. USA Rare Earth and the White House didn't immediately respond to requests for comment from Barron's. Other rare earths stocks also were getting a boost: MP Materials gained 5% and Ramaco Resources added 4.4%.

Micron Technology slid 1.6% after Reuters reported that Samsung Electronics was set to supply Nvidia with next-generation memory chips. Nvidia declined to comment on the report. Samsung didn't immediately respond to a request for comment from Barron's on Monday. Micron is the chief rival of South Korea's Samsung and SK Hynix in making high-bandwidth memory chips chips, which are crucial for the latest artificial-intelligence processors.

CoreWeave jumped 9.2%. The AI cloud company rose following the announcement of an expanded partnership with Nvidia, which includes a $2 billion investment from the chip maker.

Intel fell 2.1%. Shares dropped 17% on Friday after the company issued a downbeat outlook. Intel said it expects losses to continue into the first quarter from the fourth quarter as the company feels the impact of surging memory component costs. The stock's decline Friday erased $46 billion in market capitalization and marked its worst one-day percentage drop since early August 2024.

Advanced Micro Devices fell 1.2%, putting the chip maker on course to snap a nine-session winning streak. AMD has gained 28% over that span, with investors growing more optimistic about the chip maker's business prospects after assessing the financial results of rival Intel.

Newmont and Freeport-McMoRan gained 3.7% and 4.7%, respectively. The mining stocks rallied after gold futures topped $5,000 an ounce for the first time, with investors piling into safe-haven assets due to the prospect of another government shutdown and the threat of additional tariffs on Canada.

Revolution Medicines sank 21%. Merck is no longer in talks to buy the cancer-drug biotech, The Wall Street Journal reported, citing people familiar with the matter. Merck and Revolution Medicines didn't immediately respond to requests for comment from Barron's.

Quantum-computing company IonQ agreed to buy chip maker SkyWater Technology for $1.8 billion. IonQ will acquire the semiconductor foundry for $35 a share in a cash-and-stock transaction that is subject to a collar, or a financial safety net used to protect buyers and sellers from share-price fluctuations. IonQ gained 2.5%, and Skywater rose 7.9% to $33.78.

Sarepta Therapeutics rose 4.9% after the biotech unveiled positive top-line results for a study measuring the efficacy of Elevidys, its blockbuster treatment for Duchenne muscular dystrophy.

Write to Mackenzie Tatananni at mackenzie.tatananni@barrons.com and George Glover at george.glover@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Dollar under fire again as investors reassess Trump policies, geopolitical risk
data.description:
Key points:
  • Trump's policies create volatility, hurt dollar, investors say
  • Fed expected to cut rates, undermining dollar appeal
  • Investors diversifying away from U.S. as geopolitics heats up

By Amanda Cooper, Dhara Ranasinghe and Samuel Indyk

The dollar is coming under fire again in the first few turbulent weeks of 2026 as a growing range of factors — including Washington's desire for a weaker dollar — prompts a rethink of investors' optimistic assumptions for a period of stability for the currency.

The dollar on Monday was heading for its biggest three-day slide against a basket of major currencies since last April , when U.S. President Donald Trump's "Liberation Day" tariffs unleashed an almost unprecedented selloff in U.S. assets.

In his first year in office, Trump's erratic approach to trade and international diplomacy, his attacks on the Federal Reserve that undermine its independence, and huge increases in public spending pushed the dollar down 10%.

The dollar is again underperforming other major currencies including the euro, sterling and Swiss franc.

WHIRLWIND RATE OF CHANGE

"There are a number of factors coming together," said Seema Shah, chief global strategist at Principal Asset Management, which manages just over $600 billion worth of assets.

"I don't think this is a 'Sell America' trade, but the fundamentals are coming together, and faster than expected."

Just this month, Trump has threatened to take control of Greenland, slap more tariffs on European allies over the matter, moved to criminally indict Fed Chair Jerome Powell, and overseen an operation to seize the president of Venezuela. On Saturday, he threatened Canada with an effective trade embargo.

While he has backed down on his threats over Greenland and European tariffs, and markets have shaken off the strike on Venezuela, the backdrop is tense.

Market measures of volatility are still running hot and bond market sentiment is fragile, not least because of an aggressive selloff in Japanese government debt that could spill over into Treasuries, while gold's relentless scaling of new records is a sign investors are seeking alternative safe-havens.

Trump's domestic policies, including an aggressive crackdown on illegal immigration that has killed two U.S. citizens this month and sparked protests, could prompt another government shutdown this month.

What's more, the Fed is still expected to cut interest rates at least twice this year, while other major central banks are pausing or could even hike rates.

This alone makes the dollar less appealing to investors, who could opt to put their money somewhere where lending rates are rising.

Powell, who has resisted pressure from Trump for faster rate cuts, steps down in May. Online betting markets now attach a 50% chance to BlackRock’s Rick Rieder, an advocate of lower rates like the president, being the likely successor, up from less than 10% a week ago, adding to dollar weakness.

TIME TO MOVE ON

Global equities, meanwhile, roared higher last year, thanks in large part to enthusiasm over artificial intelligence. The performance of the S&P 500 since Trump's inauguration has lagged that of other markets. The index has risen by around 15% since then, compared with a 95% surge in Seoul's Kospi index , a 40% rise in Tokyo's Nikkei and a near 30% gain in Shanghai's main index .

"At the margin, asset managers are keen to continue to diversify away from the U.S. It's clear that many had been excessively, or felt they were excessively, overweight U.S. markets," Chris Scicluna, Daiwa Capital Markets economist, said.

Trump has repeatedly said tariffs are necessary to address trade imbalances, with a focus on currencies of Asian countries with which the U.S. has large trade deficits.

On Friday, the Bank of Japan, together with the New York Fed, was suspected of making a series of rate checks for the yen , a possible precursor to the first bout of joint Japanese-U.S. intervention in 15 years to boost the Japanese currency.

Even with the subsequent yen surge, the Japanese currency is still down around 13% against the dollar in the last year.

TRADE-WEIGHTED DOLLAR FARING MORE STRONGLY

On a trade-weighted basis, however, the dollar has only lost around 5.3% in the last 12 months, based on an index calculated by the Bank for International Settlements.

Investors are becoming more concerned about their dollar exposure, with last year's decline more down to cyclical factors such as growth moderating, said Nomura's head of G10 FX strategy Dominic Bunning.

"The difference to me (this year) is that the policies the U.S. is seemingly putting in place are much more antagonistic and geopolitical as opposed to economic with tariffs," he said.

data.title: BNSF Announces $3.6 Bln Capital Investment Plan For 2026
data.description:

data.title: Trump's Tariff Threats Designed to Influence Pending USMCA Talks, Canada PM Carney Says — Update
data.description:
By Paul Vieira

OTTAWA--Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney said President Trump's tariff threats likely represent some prepositioning ahead of negotiations later this year to renew the U.S.-Mexico-Canada trade pact.

Carney said Monday he expects a robust review of the continental trade pact, known as USMCA, which the Trump administration will lead later this year. Trade analysts expect Trump and his negotiators to push for more concessions from Canada to keep the deal intact.

Trade tension between the U.S.-Canada accelerated over the past week, with Trump and key administration officials criticizing Carney for striking a trade detente with China, America's biggest rival; and for delivering remarks at the World Economic Forum largely viewed as a rebuke of the White House's aggressive bid to use economic and military might for geopolitical goals.

Trump threatened on Saturday 100% tariffs on all Canadian imports should Ottawa and Beijing strike a free-trade pact. Carney said Canada and China moved to resolve a specific tariff row dealing with Chinese electric vehicles and Canadian agricultural and food products, and Ottawa has no intention of pursuing a broad free-trade pact with Beijing.

"I think some of these comments and positioning should be viewed in the broader context" of the pending USMCA review, Carney said at an event to unveil tax-relief measures. He added Canada and the U.S. have a "remarkable economic and security relationship, but that can be improved, and that is something that can be dealt with in negotiations."

Eurasia Group, the geopolitical risk firm, said it expects the Trump administration to use Canada's decision to reduce tariffs on some China-made EVs as leverage to extract further concessions. The Canada-China agreement calls for China to send about 49,000 EVs to Canada at a tariff rate of about 6%, well below the 100% duty that Canada imposed in 2024 to align China policy with the U.S.

"If Washington treats the EV carve‑out as a precedent, expect harder lines from the U.S. on autos and batteries, a stricter views on subsidies, and broader tariffs floated as leverage," said Kevin Ford, a foreign-exchange and macro strategist at Convera, a cross-border payments and currency exchange firm.

Canada's economy is struggling under the weight of U.S. tariffs of up to 50% on sectors such as steel, aluminum, motor vehicles and forest products. Still, about 80% of Canadian exports to the U.S. are entering duty free because they comply with the terms in USMCA. The USMCA talks represent an elevated source of uncertainty, officials and economists say, leading to businesses curtailing investment and hiring plans, and increased worries among households about job security.

The Bank of Canada issues its first policy decision of 2026 on Wednesday, and is expected to keep its main interest rate unchanged at 2.25%. Economists, though, wonder whether the central bank will cast further doubt about growth given the trade environment.

Carney has made trade diversification a priority policy matter, to reduce dependence on a protectionist U.S. His speech last week in Davos, Switzerland, warned attendees that the Western-led, rules-based trading system is history, and that smaller powers like Canada need to work collectively to counter economic coercion from global hegemons. Carney didn't identify Trump in his remarks.

Carney told reporters Monday he has no regrets about his speech, even though Trump and other senior officials weren't impressed. Trump, in his Davos speech, said Canada "gets a lot of freebies from us," and that, "Canada lives because of the U.S."

"I described the current situation. You have to be honest," Carney said. "And that highlights the need to diversify our trading relationships."

Write to Paul Vieira at paul.vieira@wsj.com
